questions.question: Prove that the perpendicular from the center to a chord bisects the chord and its converse.
questions.answer: Let AB be a chord of a circle with center O. Draw OM ⟂ AB. Using ∆OMA ≅ ∆OMB (RHS), we get AM = MB. Conversely, if a line from the center bisects a chord, it is perpendicular to the chord.

questions.question: Prove that equal chords of a circle are equidistant from the center using triangle congruence.
questions.answer: Let AB = CD be equal chords. Draw perpendiculars OM ⟂ AB and ON ⟂ CD. ∆OMA ≅ ∆ONC (RHS), so OM = ON; equal chords are equidistant from center.

questions.question: Prove that angles in the same segment of a circle are equal.
questions.answer: Let chord AB subtend ∠ACD and ∠AED on the same side. Using ∆ABC ≅ ∆ABD (or isosceles triangle property), ∠ACD = ∠AED.

questions.question: Prove the angle between a tangent and a chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
questions.answer: Let tangent at A touch circle and chord AB subtend ∠ACB in alternate segment. Using radius ⟂ tangent and triangle angle sum, angle between tangent and chord = ∠ACB.

questions.question: Prove that the sum of opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°.
questions.answer: Let ABCD be cyclic. Using angles subtended by same chord (∠ABD = ∠ACD) and triangle angle sum, ∠A + ∠C = 180°, ∠B + ∠D = 180°.

questions.question: Prove that tangents drawn from an external point are equal in length.
questions.answer: Let P be external point, PA and PB tangents. Join OP. ∆OPA ≅ ∆OPB (RHS), hence PA = PB.

questions.question: Prove that the product of lengths of two segments of a secant from an external point equals the square of the tangent.
questions.answer: Let P be external, PT tangent, PA·PB secant segments. Using similar triangles formed by tangents and secant, PT² = PA·PB.

questions.question: Prove that equal chords of a circle subtend equal angles at the center and its converse.
questions.answer: Let AB = CD, join center O to endpoints. ∆OAB ≅ ∆OCD ⇒ ∠AOB = ∠COD. Conversely, if ∠AOB = ∠COD, then AB = CD.

questions.question: Prove that the perpendicular bisector of a chord passes through the center using coordinate geometry.
questions.answer: Place circle at origin, chord along line y = k. Midpoint M of chord, perpendicular line slope -1/m passes through origin. Hence perpendicular bisector passes through center.

questions.question: Prove that the radius divides a chord into two equal parts if it passes through the midpoint.
questions.answer: Draw radius OM to midpoint of chord AB. Triangles ∆OMA ≅ ∆OMB (RHS), hence AM = MB; radius divides chord equally.

questions.question: Prove that angles subtended by the same chord on the same side are equal using circle theorems.
questions.answer: Chord AB subtends ∠ACD and ∠AED. Using angle at center property and alternate segment theorem, ∠ACD = ∠AED.

questions.question: Prove that a quadrilateral is cyclic if a pair of opposite angles sum to 180°.
questions.answer: Let ∠A + ∠C = 180°. Draw circle through A, B, D. Angle sum property ensures C lies on circle; hence quadrilateral cyclic.

questions.question: Prove that tangents at the ends of a diameter are parallel.
questions.answer: Tangents at ends of diameter perpendicular to radius, both perpendicular to same line (diameter), hence parallel.

questions.question: Prove that the exterior angle of a cyclic quadrilateral equals the interior opposite angle.
questions.answer: Extend side of quadrilateral, apply exterior angle theorem and angle sum properties; exterior angle = opposite interior angle.

questions.question: Prove that a circle passing through three vertices of a triangle is unique.
questions.answer: Perpendicular bisectors of two sides intersect at a point O, which is equidistant from all vertices. Draw circle with O as center; unique circle passes through all vertices.

questions.question: Prove the tangent-secant theorem using triangle similarity.
questions.answer: For tangent PT and secant PAB, draw radius to tangent. Triangles ∆PTC ∼ ∆PAB ⇒ PT² = PA·PB.

questions.question: Prove that the sum of angles in a triangle is 180° using a circle.
questions.answer: Draw circle passing through vertices of triangle, extend side to diameter, use angle in semicircle property to show sum of angles = 180°.

questions.question: Prove that a line joining external point to center forms equal angles with tangents drawn.
questions.answer: Triangles formed by radii to points of tangency and external point are congruent, hence angles with line to center are equal.

questions.question: Prove that the perpendicular from the center to a chord divides the circle into two equal arcs.
questions.answer: Perpendicular bisects chord, and by symmetry, corresponding arcs are equal.

questions.question: Prove that angles subtended by a diameter at any point of the circle are right angles.
questions.answer: Use triangle with diameter as hypotenuse, apply Pythagoras theorem or semicircle property to show right angle.

questions.question: Prove that the sum of angles of a cyclic quadrilateral using chord properties.
questions.answer: Use angles subtended by chords and triangle properties to show opposite angles sum to 180°.

questions.question: Prove that the external angle of a cyclic quadrilateral equals the opposite interior angle using circle theorems.
questions.answer: Extend side, use angle at center and alternate segment theorem to show exterior angle = opposite interior angle.

questions.question: Prove that if a chord bisects a diameter, the chord is perpendicular to the diameter.
questions.answer: Let chord intersect diameter at midpoint. Triangles formed are congruent; hence chord ⟂ diameter.

questions.question: Prove the alternate segment theorem using triangle properties.
questions.answer: Consider tangent at A and chord AB. Draw triangle with another point on circle, use ∆ properties and angle sum to prove theorem.

questions.question: Prove that the circumcircle of a triangle is unique and passes through all vertices using perpendicular bisectors.
questions.answer: Perpendicular bisectors of two sides intersect at center O, equidistant from all vertices. Circle with center O and radius OA passes through all vertices; uniqueness follows.
